rationale: The citing paper uses Okada (2005) to support a key theoretical premise in its argument: hedonic/luxury options are harder to justify than utilitarian/necessity options. That claim is then used to explain why luxury choices carry guilt or negative self-attributions, which the licensing mechanism is proposed to reduce. Although the citation appears briefly, it plays a clear framing role in hypothesis motivation rather than serving as a generic literature-list mention.
relation_description: Khan and Dhar draw on Okada (2005) as prior evidence that justification pressure disadvantages luxury/hedonic choices relative to necessities/utilitarian choices. They build their licensing account on this premise by proposing that a prior virtuous act reduces those negative self-attributions and raises luxury choice likelihood.
